content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquando_1"> ,<gloss>at last</gloss>, implying impatience, here assumed as a kind of apology to his hearers for the length of his account. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Marcello_1">: M. Claudius Marcellus, of a noble plebeian family (all the other families of the Claudian <gloss>gens</gloss> were patrician), was the ablest general the Romans had in the early years of the Second Punic War, but illiterate and cruel. His capture of Syracuse was in <date when="-0212">B.C. 212</date>. He was killed in battle <date when="-0208">B.C. 208</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperatoris_1">: this title, properly belonging to every holder of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>, was by usage assumed by the commander only after his first considerable victory. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cohortem_1"> ,<gloss>train</gloss> of courtiers, etc.: the provincial magistrates, representing the Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>, had almost a royal suite, as well as other insignia of royalty. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omitto_2">: a good example of the rhetorical device known as <foreign xml:lang="lat">praeteritio</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locis_2">, i.e. in the other speeches of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Accusatio</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="clausus_fuisset_2">: Marcellus had been obliged to starve out the city. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cilicum_2">: Cilicia was the chief seat of the organized bands of pirates who ruled the Mediterranean at this time. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illis_rebus_2">, i.e. the plunder of temples, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maximam_3">: the circuit of its walls was about 180 <foreign xml:lang="lat"> stadia</foreign> = more than 20 miles. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_aditu_3"> limits </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeclaro_aspectum_3"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_aedificatione_3">, etc., i.e. enclosed by the buildings of the city. Ancient harbors (as at Athens) were often at a considerable distance from the city. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conjunguntur_3">: Ortygia (or <foreign xml:lang="lat">Insula</foreign>), the site of the original town, had an independent harbor on each side connected by a narrow channel. This channel is the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exitus_3"> mentioned. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Hieronis_4">: Hiero II, king of Syracuse (<date when="-0270">B.C. 270</date> to about 216), who was during most of his reign a steadfast ally of Rome. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Dianae_4">: the quail, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄρτυξ</foreign> (whence the name <gloss> Ortygia</gloss>), was sacred to Diana (Artemis). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istius_4">, i.e. of Verres. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Arethusa_4">: for the myth of Arethusa and Alpheus, see Ovid, Met. 5.573-641; Gayley, <gloss>Classic Myths</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Achradina_5">, the plain and table-land north of Ortygia </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prytaneum_5">: the building in which the city was conceived to have its home. Here was the hearth, sacred to Vesta, whence colonists carried the sacred fire to kindle a new hearth in the <gloss>prytaneum</gloss> of their new home. It was also used for courts of justice, public banquets, etc. Such public buildings were usually grouped round the forum in the centre of the city. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbis_5">, i.e. Achradina. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perpetua_5"> ,<gloss>running its whole length</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="continentur_5"> ,<gloss>are lined in continuous rows</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gymnasium_5">: the place for exercise and baths, with porticos, groves, and halls. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="coaedificata_5"> ,<gloss>built up</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Neapolis_5">, i.e. <q rend="double">the new city.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_summam_5"> ,<gloss>at the highest point of which</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pulchritudinem_6">: the English would put it less abstractly, — <gloss>this beautiful city</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_6"> ,<gloss>in respect to</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="habuit_rationem_6"> ,<gloss>had regard for the right of victory</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deportare_6">: a Roman custom, imitated in the nineteenth century by Napoleon. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possent_6">, subj. of characteristic. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="humanitatis_6"> ,<gloss>the part of humanity</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Honoris_7"> etc the worship of abstractions was a characteristic of the Roman religion Marcellus restored the temple of Honor and built the temple of Virtus, the two were so connected that in common parlance they were referred to as a single edifice </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_aedibus_7">, i.e. in his own house in town </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornamento_7">, i.e. as being free from stolen treasures </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deum_nullum_7"> translate, in order to keep the emphasis of the position, <gloss>of the gods not one</gloss> (i e not a single statue) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comparetis_7">, i e in renown and in personal character </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pacem_bello_7">, etc implying that the administration of Verres in time of peace was worse than the armed capture by Marcellus </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="forum_dictionem_7"> ,<gloss>law and justice</gloss> the forum is mentioned as being the place where the praetor administered justice; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="juris_dictio_7"> was his special function (see note on Verres, 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.12">12</ref>, p.32, 1.13). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aedis_Minervae_8">: the illustration shows how this ancient temple of Minerva in Syracuse has been made over into a church. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_bello_8">: translate <gloss>though in war, still</gloss>, etc. The particle tamen (<q rend="double">still</q>) often suggests a concession (<q rend="double">although</q>), not expressed, but loosely implied in the context or the circumstances; here the implied concession is <q rend="double">though the rights of an enemy in war are unlimited.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religionum_8"> ,<gloss>things sacred</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consuetudinis_8">, i.e. things hallowed by use. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Agathocli_8">: tyrant of Syracuse, <date from="-0317" to="-0289"> <date when="-0317">B.C. 317</date>-289</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="visendum_8">: see Manil., sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 40">40</ref>,<gloss>p.85, l</gloss>. 6, note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profana_fecisset_8">: the Romans had a formula by which they called away (<foreign xml:lang="lat">evocare</foreign>) and gained over to their side the tutelary deities of any cities they were besieging. Of course, the temples of these gods then lost all their sanctity and became profane buildings. With the same idea the true name of Rome and that of its tutelar divinity were said to be kept as a mystery, lest they should become known to an enemy, who might thus disarm the city of its protector. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_8"> belongs with sacra religiosaque. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_9"> ,<gloss>what</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deberet_9">: subj. of characteristic. <pb n="320"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_erant_9"> ,<gloss>upon which were represented</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognitione_formarum_9"> ,<gloss>acquaintance with their features</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_9">: see note on p.52, i. 4. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_cupidum_10"> ,<gloss>that I am so eager</gloss> (in appos. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">quod</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illi_10">, i.e. the Greeks, as being over-fond of art. The Romans were inclined to look down upon culture and the fine arts as being less manly than politics and war; cf. the famous passage in the Aeneid, 6. 846-853. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="argumenta_10"> ,<gloss>subjects</gloss> or <gloss>stories</gloss> (in relief) ; cf. Aeneid, vi. 20, and note. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gorgonis_10">: the head of Medusa, a favorite subject of ancient art. See Fig. 10, from an ancient marble mask. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bullas_10">, i.e. knobs. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_nomine_11"> ,<gloss>at this item</gloss> (i.e. wondering why Verres should have taken these). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commoveri_11"> ,<gloss>were surprised</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="satis_esset_11">, i.e. they were only curiosities. Any natural object which was, in the view of the ancients, out of the common order of nature was regarded as a <foreign xml:lang="lat">monstrum</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">prodigium</foreign>, and as therefore associated in some w-ay with the gods ; hence such objects were frequently dedicated in temples. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_11"> merely repeats </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hastas_11"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haberet_12">: §<ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 265; H. 559, 4 (484. v). H.-B. 503. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nimirum_12"> (continuing the irony), <gloss>of course</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nostrum_12"> ,<gloss>of us</gloss> (emph.), opp. to <gloss>Verres</gloss>.<pb n="321"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eat_12"> (hort. subj.), <gloss>must go</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_Felicitatis_12">: the temple of <foreign xml:lang="lat">Felicitas</foreign> was adorned with the spoils of conquered Corinth. Catulus had adorned his temple of <foreign xml:lang="lat">Fortuna</foreign>, and Metellus his portico, with splendid works of art. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istorum_12">, Verres and his friends. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Tusculanum_12"> ,<gloss>villa at Tusculum</gloss> (about 15 miles southeast of Rome) where the wealthy Romans, Hortensius among the rest, had splendid country-houses. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornatum_12">, i.e. as it was on festal days. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commodarit_12"> ,<gloss>lent</gloss>: such works of art were often placed temporarily in the Forum; cf. our modern <q rend="double">loan exhibitions.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="operari_12"> ,<gloss>mere day-laborer</gloss>: said in contempt of Verres’s pretensions to culture. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studia_12"> ,<gloss>fine</gloss> <gloss>tastes</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delicias_12"> ,<gloss>luxurious pleasures</gloss> (both ironical). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appositior_12"> ,<gloss>better fitted</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_ferenda_12">, etc., <gloss>to carry</gloss> (as a porter) <gloss>than to carry off</gloss> (as a connoisseur): a sarcasm on Verres’s coarse and heavy build. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Graeculus_13">: in contemptuous allusion to his pretence of taste. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subtiliter_judicat_13"> ,<gloss>is a fine connoisseur of</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_13"> ,<gloss>now</gloss> (as it is) opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_13"> . . .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tulisset_13"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parinum_14"> (corrupt and meaningless): the common reading is parvum; perhaps the old conjecture Parium, <gloss>of Parian marble</gloss>, is best. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Libero_patre_14">: not <gloss>Liber his father</gloss>, but <gloss>father Liber</gloss>, pater being a common attribute of Liber as well as of Mars and other gods. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Liberi_filius_14"> (1.6) is spurious. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Jovem_15">: the statue was of Zeus <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὖριος</foreign>, god of favorable weather, identified, from some fancied resemblance, with <foreign xml:lang="lat">Juppiter imperator</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suo_15">: the emphatic position continues the emphasis on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Jovem_15_2"> .<pb n="322"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Flamininus_15">: T. Quinctius Flamininus, who defeated Philip of Macedon at Cynoscephalae, <date when="-0197">B.C. 197</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ore_15">: the Thracian Bosporus, the strait extending from Constantinople to the Black Sea, about 1 7 miles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Capitolio_15">: the Capitol, or Temple of Jupiter Capitolinus, had three <foreign xml:lang="lat">cellae</foreign>, or chapels, sacred to the Capitolian triad, Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva. This was now the most illustrious temple, <q rend="double">the earthly abode,</q> of Jupiter. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incolae_16"> ,<gloss>residents</gloss>, i.e. persons of foreign birth who made Syracuse their home, without having obtained citizenship ; advenae (next line), <gloss>visitors</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adventu_17">, abl. of cause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mensas_Delphicas_17">: tables with three legs, like the Delphic tripod (see Figs. 13, 14); vasa Corinthia were made of a kind of bronze, of peculiar beauty and very costly. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aedis_Minervae_8">: the illustration shows how this ancient temple of Minerva in Syracuse has been made over into a church. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_bello_8">: translate <gloss>though in war, still</gloss>, etc. The particle tamen (<q rend="double">still</q>) often suggests a concession (<q rend="double">although</q>), not expressed, but loosely implied in the context or the circumstances; here the implied concession is <q rend="double">though the rights of an enemy in war are unlimited.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religionum_8"> ,<gloss>things sacred</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consuetudinis_8">, i.e. things hallowed by use. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Agathocli_8">: tyrant of Syracuse, <date from="-0317" to="-0289"> <date when="-0317">B.C. 317</date>-289</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="visendum_8">: see Manil., sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 40">40</ref>,<gloss>p.85, l</gloss>. 6, note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profana_fecisset_8">: the Romans had a formula by which they called away (<foreign xml:lang="lat">evocare</foreign>) and gained over to their side the tutelary deities of any cities they were besieging. Of course, the temples of these gods then lost all their sanctity and became profane buildings. With the same idea the true name of Rome and that of its tutelar divinity were said to be kept as a mystery, lest they should become known to an enemy, who might thus disarm the city of its protector. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_8"> belongs with sacra religiosaque. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_9"> ,<gloss>what</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deberet_9">: subj. of characteristic. <pb n="320"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_erant_9"> ,<gloss>upon which were represented</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognitione_formarum_9"> ,<gloss>acquaintance with their features</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_9">: see note on p.52, i. 4. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_cupidum_10"> ,<gloss>that I am so eager</gloss> (in appos. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">quod</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illi_10">, i.e. the Greeks, as being over-fond of art. The Romans were inclined to look down upon culture and the fine arts as being less manly than politics and war; cf. the famous passage in the Aeneid, 6. 846-853. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="argumenta_10"> ,<gloss>subjects</gloss> or <gloss>stories</gloss> (in relief) ; cf. Aeneid, vi. 20, and note. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gorgonis_10">: the head of Medusa, a favorite subject of ancient art. See Fig. 10, from an ancient marble mask. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bullas_10">, i.e. knobs. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_nomine_11"> ,<gloss>at this item</gloss> (i.e. wondering why Verres should have taken these). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commoveri_11"> ,<gloss>were surprised</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="satis_esset_11">, i.e. they were only curiosities. Any natural object which was, in the view of the ancients, out of the common order of nature was regarded as a <foreign xml:lang="lat">monstrum</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">prodigium</foreign>, and as therefore associated in some w-ay with the gods ; hence such objects were frequently dedicated in temples. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_11"> merely repeats </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hastas_11"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haberet_12">: §<ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 265; H. 559, 4 (484. v). H.-B. 503. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nimirum_12"> (continuing the irony), <gloss>of course</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nostrum_12"> ,<gloss>of us</gloss> (emph.), opp. to <gloss>Verres</gloss>.<pb n="321"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eat_12"> (hort. subj.), <gloss>must go</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_Felicitatis_12">: the temple of <foreign xml:lang="lat">Felicitas</foreign> was adorned with the spoils of conquered Corinth. Catulus had adorned his temple of <foreign xml:lang="lat">Fortuna</foreign>, and Metellus his portico, with splendid works of art. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istorum_12">, Verres and his friends. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Tusculanum_12"> ,<gloss>villa at Tusculum</gloss> (about 15 miles southeast of Rome) where the wealthy Romans, Hortensius among the rest, had splendid country-houses. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornatum_12">, i.e. as it was on festal days. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commodarit_12"> ,<gloss>lent</gloss>: such works of art were often placed temporarily in the Forum; cf. our modern <q rend="double">loan exhibitions.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="operari_12"> ,<gloss>mere day-laborer</gloss>: said in contempt of Verres’s pretensions to culture. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studia_12"> ,<gloss>fine</gloss> <gloss>tastes</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delicias_12"> ,<gloss>luxurious pleasures</gloss> (both ironical). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appositior_12"> ,<gloss>better fitted</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_ferenda_12">, etc., <gloss>to carry</gloss> (as a porter) <gloss>than to carry off</gloss> (as a connoisseur): a sarcasm on Verres’s coarse and heavy build. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Graeculus_13">: in contemptuous allusion to his pretence of taste. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subtiliter_judicat_13"> ,<gloss>is a fine connoisseur of</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_13"> ,<gloss>now</gloss> (as it is) opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_13"> . . .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tulisset_13"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parinum_14"> (corrupt and meaningless): the common reading is parvum; perhaps the old conjecture Parium, <gloss>of Parian marble</gloss>, is best. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Libero_patre_14">: not <gloss>Liber his father</gloss>, but <gloss>father Liber</gloss>, pater being a common attribute of Liber as well as of Mars and other gods. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Liberi_filius_14"> (1.6) is spurious. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Jovem_15">: the statue was of Zeus <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὖριος</foreign>, god of favorable weather, identified, from some fancied resemblance, with <foreign xml:lang="lat">Juppiter imperator</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suo_15">: the emphatic position continues the emphasis on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Jovem_15_2"> .<pb n="322"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Flamininus_15">: T. Quinctius Flamininus, who defeated Philip of Macedon at Cynoscephalae, <date when="-0197">B.C. 197</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ore_15">: the Thracian Bosporus, the strait extending from Constantinople to the Black Sea, about 1 7 miles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Capitolio_15">: the Capitol, or Temple of Jupiter Capitolinus, had three <foreign xml:lang="lat">cellae</foreign>, or chapels, sacred to the Capitolian triad, Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva. This was now the most illustrious temple, <q rend="double">the earthly abode,</q> of Jupiter. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incolae_16"> ,<gloss>residents</gloss>, i.e. persons of foreign birth who made Syracuse their home, without having obtained citizenship ; advenae (next line), <gloss>visitors</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adventu_17">, abl. of cause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mensas_Delphicas_17">: tables with three legs, like the Delphic tripod (see Figs. 13, 14); vasa Corinthia were made of a kind of bronze, of peculiar beauty and very costly. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="levia_contemnenda_18">: cf. note on <ref n="pl53.30">p. 53, l. 30</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fanorum_18"> ,<gloss>shrines</gloss>: the word indicates the consecrated spot rather than the temple or altar erected upon it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_forte_19">: introducing, as usual, an absurd supposition. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desierunt_19"> ,<gloss>ceased</gloss>, i.e. by the transference of the courts to the Senators. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Crasso_19">: L. Licinius Crassus, the famous orator, and Quintus Scaevola, <gloss>pontifex maximus</gloss>, the famous jurist and statesman, were close friends, and colleagues in nearly every office. They were curule aediles, <date when="-0103">B.C. 103</date>, and gave the first exhibition of lion-fights. The splendor of their aedileship was the work of Crassus, a man of elegant and luxurious tastes, while Scaevola was moderate and simple in his habits. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Claudio_19">: probably a brother of Claudia, the wife of Tiberius Gracchus. In his aedileship, <date when="-0099">B.C. 99</date>, he exhibited fights of elephants. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commercium_19">: Crassus and Claudius would have bought such objects of art if anybody could have done it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuisse_19">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">commercium</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="referri_20"> ,<gloss>be entered</gloss>, has for subject </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pretio_abalienasse_20"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rebus_istis_20"> ,<gloss>things of that sort</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="apud_illos_20">, i.e. the Greeks generally. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="merere_velle_21"> ,<gloss>would take</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_21"> ,<gloss>that famous</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Tarentinos_21">: Tarentum was the largest Greek city in Italy, a colony of Sparta, founded in the eighth century B.C., subjugated by Rome just after the invasion of Pyrrhus, <date when="-0272">B.C. 272</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cnidios_Coos_21">: observe the chiasm. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="buculam_21">: the celebrated bronze cow of Myron. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="longum_21"> est, <gloss>it would be tedious</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c) ; B. 304, 3 ; G. 254, R.1 ; H. 525, 2 (476,5); H.-B. 582, 3, b. </gloss>
